title: Dog Swallowed a Corn Cob: Urgent Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a corn cob, it is a high-urgency veterinary emergency. Learn why cobs cause blockages and why you must seek help immediately.
content_html:
What Happens When a Dog Eats a Corn Cob?

While corn itself is safe for dogs, the cob is a significant health hazard. The inner core of a corn cob is made of dense, fibrous material that is completely indigestible by a dog's stomach acid. Due to its cylindrical shape and rough texture, it is the perfect size to get stuck in the narrow passage of the small intestine, leading to a complete gastrointestinal blockage.

Is it a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes, this is a high-urgency situation. Ingestion of a corn cob is considered a life-threatening emergency because they almost never pass through a dog's system naturally. You should contact an emergency veterinarian or your primary clinic immediately. Time is of the essence; if caught early, a vet may be able to induce vomiting or perform an endoscopy. If the cob moves into the intestines, major abdominal surgery is typically the only way to save the dog's life.

Why a Photo of the Cob Helps Triage

If you have the other half of the cob or a similar one from the same batch, take a quick photo of it next to a reference item like a ruler or a standard key. Showing this photo to the triage team allows the veterinarian to assess the dimensions and texture of the foreign body. This information helps them determine if the object is likely to cause an immediate obstruction and assists in planning the necessary diagnostic imaging or surgical approach.

faqs.question: Can a dog pass a corn cob on its own?
faqs.answer: It is extremely rare and highly unlikely for a dog to pass a corn cob. Their size and shape are perfectly suited to lodge in the intestines, making them one of the most common causes of surgical foreign body removal in dogs.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a blockage?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include repetitive vomiting, lethargy, loss of appetite, abdominal tenderness, and an inability to keep down water. However, you should not wait for these symptoms to appear before seeking veterinary care.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: No. You should never induce vomiting at home without direct supervision or instructions from a veterinarian. Corn cobs are large and can become a choking hazard or cause esophageal damage if they are brought back up.
